coredata.dc:title: On-site, rapid and visual determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of metal-organic frameworks and CdTe QDs
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Yuning
Liu, Wei
Cao, Jie
Wu, Yiwei
coredata.dc:description: A ratiometric sensor of MOF/ CdTe QDs was constructed by physically mixing CdTe QDs (λ em = 605 nm) with MOF (Fe-MIL-88NH2, λ em = 425 nm). Under an UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm), orange-red CdTe QDs is gradually quenched with increasing levels of Hg2+ or Cu2+, while blue MOF is unchanged, accompanying with a visual change from bright pink to blue fluorescence. Hence, a new method for determining Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor is established, whose detection limits are 0.22 ng mL−1 for Hg2+ (2.1% RSD) and 0.26 ng mL−1 for Cu2+ (1.1% RSD), respectively. In addition, on-site, rapid and visual determination of Hg2+/Cu2+ in red wine without any pretreatment is realized by using a MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor. For Hg2+ and Cu2+, the semi-quantitative detection ranges of the paper-based sensor are both 10 ~ 5000 ng mL−1, implying that it has great application value in analyzing Hg2+ and Cu2+ in liquid samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cu2+ and Hg2+
MOF/CdTe QDs
Paper-based sensor
Ratiometric fluorescence sensor
Visual detection

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body acknowledge affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid otherkwds primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of Hg2+/Cu2+ by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
Preparation of MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor
Visual detection of Hg2+/Cu2+ standard solution by MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor
On-site, visual and rapid determination of Hg2+/Cu2+ in red wine by paper-based sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
Principle of determination visually of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
Characterization of MOF, CdTe QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
Construction of visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
Effect of pH on MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
Analytical performance of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
On-site, visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Illustrate of the procedure for the formation of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor MOF/CdTe QDs and the visual detection principle for Hg2+ and Cu2+.

Properties characterization of MOF (Mag = 20.0 KX), CdTe QDs (Mag = 40.0 KX), and MOF/CdTe QDs (Mag = 20.0 KX). Fluorescence spectra of MOF (d), CdTe QDs (e) and fluorescence emission spectrum of MOF, CdTe QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs at the same concentration (f). All the pictures were taken upon excitation of 365 nm UV lamp.

(a) Selectivity of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor toward various metal ions. (b) Effect of maskers of DDTC and NH4OH in analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively. “I 425/I 605” represents the the fluorescence intensity of MOF λ em = 425 nm / the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs λ em = 605 nm. [M n
+]: 40 ng mL−1, DDTC: 500 ng mL−1, NH4OH: 3.85 × 104 ng mL−1.

(a) Fluorescence emission spectrum of assembled sensor MOF/CdTe QDs after add with different concentrations of Hg2+ (0–24.0 ng mL−1) (a), (0–40.0 ng mL−1) Cu2+ (b), respectively. All the images were taken upon excitation of 365 nm UV lamp. The insets show a plot of fluorescence intensity relative value (I 425/I 605) versus the concentration of Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively, λ ex = 330 nm.

(a) Experimental procedure of visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ based on paper fluorescence sensor. (b) The fluorescence images of paper-based sensor visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine with different dilution times, respectively. All the images were taken upon excitation of 365 nm UV lamp. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Analytical results of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in actual samples.

head.ce:title: On-site, rapid and visual determination of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of metal-organic frameworks and CdTe QDs

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, Institute for Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry
Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Collaborative Innovation Center for Rare Metal Chemistry, Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis & Reuse Technology, Institute for Advanced Materials, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Hubei Normal University, Huangshi 435002, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A ratiometric sensor of MOF/ CdTe QDs was constructed by physically mixing CdTe QDs (λ em = 605 nm) with MOF (Fe-MIL-88NH2, λ em = 425 nm). Under an UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm), orange-red CdTe QDs is gradually quenched with increasing levels of Hg2+ or Cu2+, while blue MOF is unchanged, accompanying with a visual change from bright pink to blue fluorescence. Hence, a new method for determining Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor is established, whose detection limits are 0.22 ng mL−1 for Hg2+ (2.1% RSD) and 0.26 ng mL−1 for Cu2+ (1.1% RSD), respectively. In addition, on-site, rapid and visual determination of Hg2+/Cu2+ in red wine without any pretreatment is realized by using a MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor. For Hg2+ and Cu2+, the semi-quantitative detection ranges of the paper-based sensor are both 10 ~ 5000 ng mL−1, implying that it has great application value in analyzing Hg2+ and Cu2+ in liquid samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry
manganese ion doped carbon quantum dots

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal pollution has become an increasingly serious environmental problem. As a typical toxic heavy metal ion, mercury ion (Hg2+) can damage the central nervous system, endocrine system and other functional barriers (Nolan & Lippard, 2008; Wang, Chen, Zhu, Yu, & Hu, 2015). Copper ion (Cu2+), as an essential trace element in human body, is involved in synthesis of various biological enzymes as a auxiliary group (Wang et al., 2016). However, too much Cu2+ can disrupt the balance between cells, thus leading to a series of neurodegenerative diseases, such as Alzheimer’s, Wilson’s and Parkinson’s (Yin, Leen, Snick, Boens, & Dehaen, 2010). Therefore, it is very important to develop an efficient, rapid and sensitive method to detect Hg2+ and Cu2+ in food and water body.

At present, the techniques for detecting heavy metal ions commonly include atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) (Kamyabi & Aghaei, 2017),inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry(ICP-AES) (Zhang, Shi, & Yu-Qiang, 2017),inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) (Yu, Chen, He, Wang, & Hu, 2019), atomic fluorescence spectrometry (AFS) (Azizi, Golshekan, Shariati, & Rahchamani, 2015), etc. However, these methods usually require hyphenating with a sample pretreatment technique due to low levels of Hg2+ and Cu2+ and complex matrix from real samples. They are silica magnetite mesoporous nanoparticles solidphaseextraction coupled with AAS for analyzing Cu2+ in food and natural waters (Azizi et al., 2015) and HPLC-UV-HG-AFS for Hg2+ in environmental and biological samples (Gao et al., 2018), which are tedious and contaminated easily.

In recent years, fluorescence spectrometry has been widely employed for analyzing heavy metal ions due to the advantages of simplicity, sensitivity and economy (Zhang et al., 2013). In this field, inorganic semiconductor quantum dots (QDs), an excellent fluorescence probe with high fluorescence and stability (Zhai et al., 2016), particularly attract people’s attention. It is applied to detect metal ions (Jiao, Zhang, Liang, Peng, & Lin, 2014), biomedicine (Zvaigzne, Martynov, Samokhvalov, & Nabiev, 2016), cell imaging (Wu et al., 2018) and photoelectric sensor (Amjadi, Jalili, & Manzoori, 2016) and so on. Gong et al. (2016)) successfully detected Ag+, Hg2+ and Cu2+ in environment water and food samples based on l-cysteine modified CdTe quantum dots. In addition, QDs are also usually combined with the other fluorescence probes to fabricate a ratiometric fluorescence sensor with self-correcting function, thus eliminating the interference from instrumental and experimental medium (Lu & Xu, 2017). Sun, Liu, Wu, and Liu (2015) constructed a dual photoluminescence probe for selective detection of Cu2+ with graphene QDs as detecting signal and CdTe QDs as internal standard. Yao et al. (2013) also designed a dual emissive ratiometric fluorescence probe to analyze Cu2+ visually. Jin, Mou, Zhang, Liang, and Zhang (2017) established a dual-emission nanosensor of NH2-MIL-53 (Al) andCdTe QDs to detect 6-mercaptopurine.

Metal organic framework (MOF), a new type of organic and inorganic porous material formed by metal ions and organic ligands (Gu, Yang, Chang, & Yan, 2012), has been widely used in analytical chemistry (Feng, Zhang, Zhou, & Sharma, 2018; Sun, Liu, & Li, 2015), drug targeting delivery and release (Wang, Dong, Cheng, Wan, & Zhang, 2015) and heterogeneous catalysis (Chen & Jiang, 2016). A novel high fluorescence hybrid material (Eu3+/CDs@MOF-253) has been synthesized to detect Hg2+ by Yan and Xu (2016). However, there are few reports about MOF (Fe-MIL-88NH2) as internal reference of a ratiometric fluorescence probeto detect Hg2+ and Cu2+, respectively.

In this study, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor MOF/CdTe QDs (I425/I605 ) for detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+ in drinks and water samples was fabricated by physically mixing MOF with CdTe QDs, in which CdTe QDs (λ em = 605 nm) was used for detecting signal and MOF (λ em = 425 nm) as internal standard. On account of the obvious visual effects under the UV lamp, an acetate paper-based sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs was made for on-site, rapid and semi-quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine.

Cd(CH3COO)2·2H2O (Beilian Fine Chemicals Development Co. Ltd), NaBH4, FeCl3·6H2O and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) (Tianjin Komeo Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd), 2-aminoterephthalic acid (Shanghai SAN Chemical Technology Co. Ltd) and K2TeO3 and thioglycolic acid (TGA) (Aladdin) were used as reagents in the experiments. Water-soluble CdTe QDs was prepared with a preparation method similar to that in previous researches, but it takes longer reflux time (18 h) (Gong et al., 2016). All the chemical reagents are analytically pure and could be directly used without further purification. Britton-Robinson buffer was prepared by properly mixing CH3COOH, H3BO3 and H3PO4. All used in the experiment were deionized water.

The probes were characterized by Nicolet 5700 infrared spectrometer (Nicolet, USA), ZEISS Gemini SEM 300 instrument (ZEISS, Germany), Zetasizer NANO ZS3600 (Malvern, Britain). The experiments were carried out by using the instruments, such as F-4700 fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan), U-3010 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), FLS 980 time-resolved spectroscope (Edinberge, UK) and precision digital pH meter (PB-10) (Sartorius, Germany).

2-aminoterephthalic acid of 0.126 g (0.692 mmol) and FeCl3·6H2O of 0.187 g (0.692 mmol) were quickly dissolved in 15 mL DMF and then acetic acid of 3.45 mmol was added in the mixture for performing ultrasound for 30 min. After that, the mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave of 50 mL and reacted at 120 °C for 4 h. After cooling, the precipitate was washed and centrifuged with DMF, ethanol and water in sequence. Finally, the obtained solid was dried in vacuum at 35 °C for 24 h for later use (Zhao, Liu, & Li, 2015).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Hg2+/Cu2+ by ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: MOF/CdTe QDs sensor was fabricated by mixing 50 μL MOF, 50 μL CdTe QDs and 625 μL 0.88 × 104 ng mL-1B-R buffer solution (pH 7.0). Then, Hg2+ and Cu2+ at various standard concentrations were separately added into the sensor, and diluted to 5.0 mL with deionized water, and then ultrasound for 15 min at room temperature. The spectra of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor were recorded at λ ex = 330 nm for both MOF (λ em = 425 nm) and CdTe QDs (λ em = 605 nm). The color changes were observed under the UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm).

Environmental water from Qingshan Lake in Huangshi City, Hubei Province, China as well as juice and red wine randomly obtained from local supermarket were centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min and filtrated with a microporous membrane with thickness of 0.45 μm to remove large particles. The filtrate was collected and stored in refrigerator at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: CdTe QDs of 0.5 mL, 4.0 × 104 ng mL−1 MOF of 1.0 mL and B-R buffer solution of 625 μL 0.88 × 104 ng mL−1 were mixed, and diluted to 5 mL with deionized water, and then ultrasound for 15 min at room temperature. The cellulose acetate membrane was cut into a triangle one to immerse in the above MOF/CdTe QDs solution and then it was taken out after ultrasound for 15 min. Finally, the obtained paper-based sensors were stocked in line on a clean black board.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual detection of Hg2+/Cu2+ standard solution by MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: By using a clean quartz capillary, a series of Hg2+ (10, 100, 1000, 5000 ng mL−1) and Cu2+ (10, 100, 1000, 5000 ng mL−1) were uniformly dropped into MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor. After 2 min, the paper-based sensor was observed under the UV lamp (λex = 365 nm) and photos were taken with a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site, visual and rapid determination of Hg2+/Cu2+ in red wine by paper-based sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: Two portions of red wine (5.0 mL each) were taken and added with 0.5 mL of 1.0 × 105 ng mL−1 DDTC and 0.5 mL of 3.85 × 104 ng mL−1 NH4OH to eliminate the interference of Cu2+ or Hg2+ in alone analysis. Then the mixture was diluted for 0.0, 5.0 and 10.0 times, respectively, and dropped onto the paper-based sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs by using a clean quartz. After 2 min, the resulting paper-based sensor was observed under the UV lamp (λ ex = 365 nm) and the photos were taken by a mobile phone.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principle of determination visually of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 , the ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs (bright pink) was assembled by physically mixing CdTe QDs (orange-red) and MOF (blue) to visually and sensitively detect Hg2+ and Cu2+. The ratiometric fluorescence sensor has two emission peaks at λ em = 425 nm for MOF and λ em = 605 nm for CdTe QDs both excited at 330 nm. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs can be quenched linearly with the increase of concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+, while the fluorescence intensity of MOF has almost unchanged. Therefore, by using CdTe QDs as detecting signal and MOF as internal standard, the signal of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor (I 425/I605) was obtained for separately determining Hg2+ and Cu2+. Meanwhile, under the UV lamp (λ em = 365 nm), the color of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor changed gradually from bright pink to pink, to purplishred, to purplishblue andto blue with the increase of Hg2+ and Cu2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of MOF, CdTe QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: SEM, Zeta potential, FT-IR, fluorescencespectra were used to characterize MOF, CdTe QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs sensor and the results are displayed as follows:

Fig. 2 a, b and c show the SEM images of 50 μL MOF, 50 μL CdTe QDs and the mixture of 50 μL MOF and 50 μL CdTe QDs. In Fig. 2, the amplification factors of SEM for MOF (Fig. 2a), CdTe QDs (Fig. 2b) and MOF/CdTe QDs (Fig. 2c) are 20.00, 40.00 and 20.00 KX, respectively. Because the content of MOF in Fig. 2a is higher than that in Fig. 2c, the white dots representing MOF are a little different in the two figures. As for morphologies of CdTe QDs, there is little difference in Fig. 2b and 2c.

Supplementary Figs. S1a and S1b demonstrate the FT-IR characterization analysis of TGA, MOF and CdTe QDs. In Supplementary Fig. S1a, the vibration of aromatic hydrocarbon at 1660 cm−1, 1570 cm−1, 1370 cm−1 and 1439 cm−1 and NH at 3460 cm−1 are observed. Meanwhile, the characteristic peak of –COOH (1700 cm−1) is not seen in the Fig S1, indicating that the carboxyl group has coordinated with Fe3+ and the band (Pham, Vuong, Vu, & Do, 2011). Supplementary Fig. S1b illustrates that the stretching vibration peak of -SH at 2500 ~ 2600 cm−1 in TGA disappears in CdTe QDs, suggesting that TGA was successfully modified on the surface of CdTe QDs through S-Cd bond due to the strong affinity of -SH to Cd2+ (Li, Wang, Ni, & Kokot, 2015). The S-Cd bond increases the composite fluorescence intensity by generating more radiation centers on CdTe/ Cd-SR and passivating surface defects in CdTe QDs (Khantaw, Boonmee, Tuntulani, & Ngeontae, 2013).

The results of Zeta potential in Supplementary Fig. S1c show that both MOF and CdTe QDs are electronegative (MOF −2.54 mV and CdTe QDs −1.28 mV), proving that there is no interaction between the two probes because of the electrostatic repulsion.

The excitation and emission spectra of MOF (Fig. 2d), CdTe QDs (Fig. 2e) and the fluorescence spectra of MOF, CdTe QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs sensor (Fig. 2f) demonstrate that both MOF and CdTe QDs can be excited at 330 nm, and the fluorescence intensity and the position of fluorescence peak remained basically unchanged before and after mixing, suggesting that both MOF and CdTe QDs maintain their respective optical properties and do not interact with each other.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To fabricate the visual ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/CdTe QDs, we optimized the volumes of both MOF and CdTe QDs by keeping one constant and the other changing, and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S2. It is obvious that both the fluorescence intensities of CdTe QDs and MOF increase with the volumes of CdTe QDs and MOF, while their signals remain almost unchanged when their volumes are unchanged. To get an obvious and visual color change, as seen in Supplementary Fig. S3, the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was constructed by mixing 50 μL CdTe QDs and 50 μL MOF. Effects of pH, selectivity and masking experiments on the sensor were optimized as follows.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A suitable pH is very important for a fluorescence probe owing to the protonation and deprotonation. Therefore, the fluorescence intensities of MOF, QDs and MOF/CdTe QDs sensor at pH 2.0 ~ 11.0 were studied and the results are shown in Supplementary Fig. S4. It can be observed from Fig. S4 that the maximum fluorescence intensities of CdTe QDs and MOF are found at pH 6.0 ~ 7.0 and 8.0 ~ 11.0, respectively. Whether CdTe QDs and MOF are mixed or not, they both had the same pH effects on each of them. Strong acid environment exerts negative influences on both CdTe QDs and MOF owing to the protonation (Lu & Xu, 2017), which reduces fluorescence intensity. Comprehensively, the optimal pH of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor was set at pH 7.0.

Another important factor for a fluorescence sensor is selectivity, for which it is the prerequisite for testing analytes in a real samples. Under the optimal conditions, effects of metal ions including Mg2+, Pb2+, Co2+, Ba2+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Cr6+, Zr4+, Ca2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Cu2+ on MOF/CdTe QDs sensor were separately studied. As illustrated in Fig. 3 a, except for Hg2+ and Cu2+, other metal ions exert little influence on the fluorescence intensity ratio of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor (I 425/I 605), indicating that the sensor has a good selectivity to Hg2+ and Cu2+.

What’s more, the reason why Hg2+ and Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of CdTe QDs may be that compounds of HgS and CuS are formed between -SR groups on the surface of CdTe QDs and Hg2+, Cu2+, respectively. There is stronger affinity because the Ksp values of HgS (K sp = 4 × 10-53) and CuS (K sp = 6 × 10-36) are both lower than that of CdS (K sp = 8 × 10-27). Therefore, MOF/CdTe QDs sensor has high selectivity to Hg2+ and Cu2+.

Based on the above experimental results, MOF/CdTe QDs sensor is selective to both Hg2+ and Cu2+. Hence, to accurately detect Hg2+ and Cu2+, DDTC and NH4OH were tested to eliminate the interference of each other and the results are displayed in Fig. 3 b. It is obvious that Hg2+ and Cu2+ could be separately detected by using the masking reagent DDTC for Cu2+ and NH4OH for Hg2+, respectively.

The fluorescence quenching process of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor at different temperatures (298 K, 303 K, 310 K) was investigated by adding Hg2+ and Cu2+ at different levels, and the results are expressed by Stern-Volmer equation (F 0/F = 1 + K SV[ Hg2+ or Cu2+ ]. Where F 0 and F separately represent the fluorescence intensities of CdTe QDs before and after adding Hg2+ or Cu2+, K SV denotes the quenching constant). Stern-Volmer equation (in Supplementary Fig. S5) reveals that (F 0 -F)/F linearly fits well with concentration of [Hg2+ or Cu2+] and the K SV of Hg2+ and Cu2+ increases with temperature, implying that the dynamic quenching mechanism plays a dominant role in the quenching process (Wei et al., 2018).

To further study the fluorescence quenching process, the fluorescence life of CdTe QDs was studied in presence and absence of Hg2+ and Cu2+ because only CdTe QDs acted as the detecting signal in MOF/CdTe QDs sensor. Supplementary Fig. S6 shows that the fluorescence life of CdTe QDs is shortened obviously in the presence of Hg2+ and Cu2+. This also demonstrates that the quenching mechanism of CdTe QDs by Hg2+ and Cu2+ is a dynamic process (Alam et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical performance of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor for detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+ was investigated. The results are presented in Fig. 4 . As shown, the relative fluorescence intensity ratio (I 425/I 605) has a good linear relationship with the ranges of Hg2+ (4.0–24.0 ng mL−1) and Cu2+ (4.0–40.0 ng mL−1). Their limits of detection (LODs) are 0.22 ng mL−1 for Hg2+ and 0.26 ng mL−1 for Cu2+, respectively. Analytical characteristic data of this work are listed in Supplementary Table S1.

Supplementary Table S2 demonstrates the comparison of the analytical performances of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor for detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+ with other fluorescence methods. As the same kind of method based on application different fluorescence probes in analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+, LOD of this method for Hg2+ and Cu2+ is lower than those of results (Anand & Sahoo, 2019; Hao et al., 2017; Pang et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2020; Xiao et al., 2020) and higher than work (Ding, Shen, Sun, Sun, & Liu, 2014; Lu et al., 2012). In addition, as a ratio fluorescence sensor, this method not only realizes multi-element measurement (Hg2+ and Cu2+), but also has higher accuracy and lower detection limit due to the self-correcting function of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor.

In order to further explore the feasibility of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor in detecting Hg2+ and Cu2+, contents of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in real lake water, juice and red wine samples were analyzed. As shown in Table 1 , Hg2+ and Cu2+ are both found in juice and red wine samples and the recovery rate changes from 91.6% to 108.6%, which is acceptable and satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site, visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor
ce:section.ce:para: As in Sections 2.4~2.7, theexperimentalprocedure(Fig. 5 (a))and photographs (Fig. 5(b)) are shown in Fig. 5. In Fig. 5 (b), the left column presents the visual pictures of a series of Hg2+ (10, 100, 1000, 5000 ng mL−1) and Cu2+ (10, 100, 1000, 5000 ng mL−1), while the right column is theon-site,visualphotographsofHg2+ and Cu2+ inredwine(dilutedto0.0,5.0 and 10.0times). It is obvious that the fluorescence color of MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor gradually changes from bright pink to blue with the increase of the concentration of Hg2+ and Cu2+ (Fig. 5(b) left). Similarly, the color also varied from bright pink to blue with the increase levels of Hg2+ and Cu2+ inredwine (Fig. 5(b) right). By comparing the colors in the left and right columns, the semi-quantitative contents of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine are 100 ~ 300 ng mL−1 and 100 ~ 500 ng mL−1, respectively, which coincide well with the results in Table 1 for Hg2+ (150 ng mL−1) and Cu2+ (200 ng mL−1). Therefore, a new, simple and intuitive method for on-site, visual and semi-quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor has been established successfully. The method with low cost and high speed has great prospects for on-site, convenient analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in liquid samples without any pretreatment.

In this paper, a ratiometric fluorescence sensor of MOF/ CdTe QDs was constructed to realize on-site, visual detection of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by physically mixing CdTe QDs with MOF. In analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ by MOF/ CdTe QDs sensor, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs was used as detection signal and the fluorescence intensity of MOF as reference because Hg2+ and Cu2+ could quench linearly the fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs and could not change the fluorescence intensity of MOF. Under the UV lamp, color of MOF/CdTe QDs sensor changed gradually from bright pink to blue with the increase of Hg2+ and Cu2+. Hence, MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor was made for on-site, visual and semi-quantitative analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine by comparing the colors of standard and actual sample on MOF/CdTe QDs paper-based sensor. Moreover, paper-based sensor not only has a wide semi-quantitative concentration range (10 ~ 5000 ng mL−1) for both Hg2+ and Cu2+, but also has been successfully applied to visual detection ofHg2+ and Cu2+ in red wine with satisfactory results. The method is simple, accurate and intuitive and has low cost and high speed, so it has great prospects for on-site, rapid analysis of Hg2+ and Cu2+ in liquid samples without any pretreatment.
Yuning Yang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Formal analysis, Writing - original draft. Wei Liu: Investigation, Resources. Jie Cao: Investigation, Resources. Yiwei Wu: Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This project was financially supported by Education Committee of Hubei Province (D20132501), Hubei Key Laboratory of Pollutant Analysis and Reuse Technique (Hubei Normal University) (PA180204).

ce:bib-reference.ce:label: Anand and Sahoo, 2019

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Cost-effective approach to detect Cu(II) and Hg(II) by integrating a smartphone with the colorimetric response from a NBD-benzimidazole based dyad[J]
